summary: My grandfather, Kisaburo Konichi, was born around 1865 near Yokohama, Japan. His family was connected to the samurai class before the Meiji Restoration, with his father serving as a groom to a samurai. Kisaburo came to the United States in the 1890s, initially settling in San Francisco, where he experienced the earthquake and then moved to Sacramento. Later, he moved to Los Angeles and ran a go parlor from his living room, where he was known as a skilled go player and also made posters for community events. He died in the 1930s, and I never met him personally.

Japan is where my grandparents were born and raised, specifically near Yokohama. My grandfather's family was involved with the samurai class before the Meiji Restoration, and my grandmother’s family was well off, distributing Suntory whiskey. My grandparents eventually left Japan in the late 19th and early 20th centuries to settle in the United States. The cultural traditions and language from Japan were very important in our family, especially as they maintained Japanese language and customs after moving to America.

Yokohama was the birthplace of both my grandfather Kisaburo Konichi and my grandmother Chiyo Konishi. It was a city environment where my grandmother grew up, and she never learned to cook because they often bought food from vendors. This city background was part of their early life before they immigrated to the United States. Yokohama was also where my grandmother’s family was involved in the whiskey distribution business.

The Meiji Restoration was an important historical backdrop for my grandfather’s family in Japan. Before this period, his father was a groom to a samurai, which placed them in a certain social class. The changes brought by the Meiji Restoration likely influenced my grandfather’s decision to come to the United States in the 1890s, as opportunities in Japan shifted dramatically during that time.

San Francisco was the first place my grandfather Kisaburo Konichi settled when he arrived in the United States in the 1890s. He was actually there during the famous earthquake, which frightened him so much that he left the city by ferry and then walked all the way to Sacramento. This experience marked the beginning of our family’s life in America.

Sacramento is where my grandfather lived after leaving San Francisco, and it’s where my mother, Chie Konishi, was born in 1911. My grandmother was also born nearby and lived there during her early years in the U.S. Before moving to Los Angeles, my grandfather made posters and taught Japanese in Sacramento. It was an important early home for our family in America.

My grandmother, Chiyo Konishi, was born in Yokohama in 1890 to a family involved in distributing Suntory whiskey. After her mother died when she was young, a geisha who had been helping the family married her father, giving Chiyo a half-brother. She moved to the U.S. as a young woman and lived in Sacramento before moving to Little Tokyo in Los Angeles around 1913. She never learned English but adapted well, living with us throughout my childhood and taking care of the household while my parents worked hard.

Suntory was the whiskey company connected to my grandmother Chiyo Konishi’s family in Yokohama. Her father was a distributor for Suntory whiskey in that area, which meant the family was fairly well off. This connection to Suntory was part of the family’s background before they immigrated to the United States.

Los Angeles became our family’s home around 1913 when my grandparents moved from Sacramento to Little Tokyo. My grandfather ran a go parlor from our living room, and my grandmother took care of the household. My mother and aunt grew up there, attending local schools and participating in the Japanese community. Los Angeles was where much of our family’s American life unfolded.

Little Tokyo in Los Angeles was where my grandparents lived after moving from Sacramento. It was a vibrant Japanese community where my mother and aunt grew up and attended Amelia Street School. My grandfather’s go parlor was located there, and the neighborhood supported many cultural traditions, including Japanese language use at home and community events. It was also where my mother and aunt acted in Japanese films during their childhood.

My mother, Chie Konishi, was born in Sacramento in 1911 before our family moved to Los Angeles when she was about two years old. She grew up in Little Tokyo, attending Amelia Street School where speaking Japanese was forbidden, although her father insisted on Japanese at home. She and her younger sister Michiko acted in Japanese films as children, sometimes alongside famous actors like Sessue Hayakawa. My mother worked very hard throughout her life, and her upbringing was shaped by the strictness of her father and the dual cultural worlds she navigated.

Michiko is my mother’s younger sister, born about a year after my mother, also in Sacramento. She grew up alongside my mother in Little Tokyo, Los Angeles, and shared many experiences including attending Amelia Street School and acting in Japanese films as children. Michiko was part of the family’s close-knit Japanese community during their childhood.

Amelia Street School was the public grammar school my mother and aunt attended in Little Tokyo, Los Angeles. At school, they were not allowed to speak Japanese and had to use English, which created a cultural divide since their father insisted on Japanese at home. My mother recalled the penny lunches there, where students could buy milk and dessert for just a few cents. The school was an important part of their childhood experience growing up in the Japanese American community.

Sessue Hayakawa was a fairly famous Japanese actor who appeared in some of the films my mother and aunt acted in as children during the 1910s or 1920s. My family remembers him as part of that early Japanese film scene in Los Angeles, where my relatives participated in movies that were clearly Japanese in style and costume. Although I don’t know much about the production companies, Hayakawa’s involvement connects our family to that unique cultural moment.

Venice, Los Angeles was the area where the movie studios were located where my mother and aunt went to film their Japanese movies as children. This was outside of Little Tokyo and represented a connection to the broader entertainment industry in Southern California. We still have photos of them in costume from those film projects.

Rafu Shimpo is a Japanese-language newspaper that was part of the community in Little Tokyo. While my family used a Japanese horoscope book for daily guidance, it’s possible that Rafu Shimpo or similar newspapers carried such content. The paper was an important source of news and cultural connection for Japanese Americans like my family during that time.

The Young Buddhist Women's Association was an organization my mother and aunt belonged to as part of their religious and community life in Little Tokyo. They attended Buddhist Sunday School and participated in statewide events through this group. Although my grandparents were not particularly religious, this association helped connect my mother and aunt to their cultural and spiritual heritage.
